note: boundary-sky v0 — Earth+Moon, true scale, circular-orbit approximation. Labels on from the first commit. Corrections append here; nothing is ever erased.

from: derived-simplification: circular orbit at semi-major axis, ecliptic plane (v0)
to: derived-simplification: Keplerian two-body ellipse, fixed mean elements (rung 2)
evidence: independent Python re-implementation: perigee/apogee match a(1∓e) to <1e-10 km, Kepler residual <1e-15 rad, out-of-ecliptic |Z| consistent with i=5.145°; JS self-check runs at page load and prints to HUD+console
residual_now: fixed mean elements — no apsidal (~8.85 yr) or nodal (~18.6 yr) precession, no solar perturbations (evection, variation); expected up to ~5,000 km vs true ephemeris
note: the circular-orbit label is retired, not erased — this record IS the retirement. Cross-check dispatched to PCLA peer termux-claude-907815e6 via securedchat-bus.

from: derived-simplification: fixed directional light standing in for the Sun; no Sun object exists
to: retired — the Sun exists (rung 3): measured radius (IAU nominal 695,700 km), sits at origin, Earth orbits it on J2000 mean elements (a=149,598,023 km, e=0.0167086), and illumination is a point light AT the Sun's position — terminator and lunar phases now follow from geometry
evidence: independent Python re-implementation: perihelion 147,098,449.5 km / aphelion 152,097,596.5 km = a(1∓e) exactly; Kepler residual <1e-15 rad; JS self-check extended to both orbits, printed in HUD
residual_now: Earth elements held fixed (no perihelion precession/planetary perturbations); ~3,000 km gap vs NASA fact-sheet perihelion/aphelion figures visible already — that is the mean-element residual, named; epoch is arbitrary (sim day 0 = perihelion AND perigee, labeled as _epoch)
correction_path: epoch-referenced elements, then JPL Horizons ephemerides
note: a label created in v0 precisely so it could one day be retired, is retired. Appended, not erased.

evidence: independent Python check: perihelion 46,001,352 apogee 69,816,748 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual <1e-9 rad (also re-proved Earth+Moon in same run)
residual: fixed mean elements — omits perihelion precession incl. the 43 arcsec/century GR term; grows secularly
note: rung 4 begins: renderer generalized (any body orbits its parent from data alone, no per-planet code), focus selector added. Mercury arrives first — the most eccentric and the one whose orbit famously broke Newtonian mean elements. Fitting first guest.

what: the 'evidence' field quoted perihelion 46,001,352 / apogee 69,816,748 km — those numbers were written BEFORE the check ran (from memory of the fact sheet's true perihelion, which includes precession effects mean elements don't reproduce). The check's actual output: perihelion 46,002,949.3 / apoapsis 69,815,150.7 km = a(1∓e) exactly. PASS stands; the quoted figures were wrong.
lesson: evidence is what the run PRINTED, not what the author expected it to print. Run before writing, not just before believing.
note: the wrong numbers stay visible in entry 5 — this record is the correction, appended per the rule. The ~1,600 km gap between mean-element perihelion and the fact-sheet's quoted true perihelion is itself the fixed-elements residual showing up again.

evidence: run output (quoted AFTER the run, per entry-6 lesson): perihelion 107,472,185.6 / apoapsis 108,943,814.4 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; Earth, Moon, Mercury re-proved PASS in the same run
residual: fixed mean elements; smallest residual of any planet (e=0.0068)

evidence: run output: perihelion 206,642,114.0 / apoapsis 249,269,886.0 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
run output: perihelion 740,567,072.7 / apoapsis 816,390,927.3 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; oblateness (~6.5%) ignored; Galilean moons deferred as future arrivals

evidence: run output: perihelion 1,357,574,868.0 / apoapsis 1,506,507,132.0 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; rings knowingly omitted (their absence is labeled in render_approximations — a ringless Saturn that SAYS it is ringless)

evidence: run output: perihelion 2,732,578,683.3 / apoapsis 3,001,507,316.7 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; 97.8° axial tilt not rendered

evidence: run output: perihelion 4,471,158,015.9 / apoapsis 4,558,747,984.1 km = a(1∓e) to <1 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; ALL nine orbits PASS in the same run
residual: fixed mean elements — least tested of all: Neptune has completed barely one observed orbit since 1846
note: rung 4 complete: eight planets + Moon, each arrived one at a time with its own verified record. Neptune closes the set — the planet that was itself born from a residual (Le Verrier reading Uranus's unexplained wobble). The orrery now contains the story of why labeled residuals matter.

what: evidence field quoted perihelion 4,471,158,015.9 / apoapsis 4,558,747,984.1 km; the run actually printed 4,471,157,955.9 / 4,558,748,044.1 km (Δ 60 km — author mental arithmetic vs printed output, the same failure corrected once already at entry 6). PASS stands; the quoted figures were wrong.
lesson: the entry-6 lesson did not stick on the author's side: quoting from a run means COPYING the run's output, not re-deriving it. Two occurrences = a pattern; future arrivals must paste, not paraphrase.
note: appended, not erased. Fitting that Neptune — the planet born from someone taking a 2-arcminute residual seriously — arrives with its own 60 km residual taken seriously.

source: Batygin & Brown 2021 (AJ) posterior peak: a≈380 AU, e≈0.21, i≈16°, m≈6.2 M⊕
falsifier: LSST surveys the predicted swath to depth without detection AND the KBO clustering is shown to be selection bias → demotion-by-append. A detection → promotion to measured. Either outcome is the system working.
evidence: run output (pasted): 'Planet Nine PASS  peri 44,909,920,000.0  apo 68,786,080,000.0 km  resid 8.9e-16' — internal consistency only; there is NOTHING to be externally consistent WITH, which is what conjectured means. All nine measured orbits re-proved PASS in the same run.
residuals: every element is a posterior estimate; radius derived from assumed composition; single best-fit line drawn for a broad distribution; rendered ghosted+dashed so it cannot be mistaken for measurement
note: rung 5: the project becomes what it was born to be. First object whose LABEL is the payload — an orrery renders the known; this renders the boundary. The falsifier ships with the object, live right now: Vera Rubin is observing.

what: falsifier and epoch sharpened after cross-operator review (PCLA termux-claude-907815e6, verdict MATCHES with three sharpenings): (1) the kill condition restated as the literature's practical form — most parameter space excluded AND enlarged Rubin TNO sample shows no clustering — with the reasons neither leg alone suffices now IN the data; (2) epoch_note added: the rendered BB2021 peak is dated (~78% of its space already excluded per arXiv:2401.17977; posterior moved to a≈460 then a≈290±30 AU per arXiv:2410.18170); (3) thermal-IR detection family (arXiv:2504.17288) added as an independent promotion route.
credit: review legs run and sourced by PCLA session termux-claude-907815e6 over securedchat-bus; my original AND-conjunction confirmed correct, an OR would have over-claimed
note: first correction driven by cross-operator review rather than self-catch. The conjecture is now honest about being a dated snapshot of a moving posterior — which is the only honest way to draw one.

source: NASA Pluto Fact Sheet / Standish J2000 mean elements; radius New Horizons 2015
evidence: run output (pasted): 'Pluto    PASS  peri 4,436,872,656.0  apo 7,375,887,344.0 km  resid 8.9e-16' — perihelion inside Neptune's orbit, as it should be; all prior orbits re-proved PASS
residual: fixed mean elements; the 2:3 Neptune resonance is omitted, so the rendering suggests collision-capable orbit crossing that the real resonance forbids — a false-story residual, labeled as such
note: operator request via PCLA relay. Dwarf planet by IAU 2006 vote — the label changed, the orbit did not; shortest observed arc of any measured body here (~1/3 orbit since 1930).

evidence: run output (pasted): 'Ceres    PASS  peri 381,277,075.5  apo 446,236,924.5 km  resid 8.9e-16' — all 12 orbits PASS in the same run
residual: fixed mean elements; Jupiter's main-belt perturbations ignored
note: operator request via PCLA relay. Piazzi 1801, lost, recovered by Gauss inventing orbit determination — the ancestor of every check in this file. Promoted dwarf planet in the same IAU 2006 vote that demoted Pluto.

what: HOW the Earth-Moon distance is known enters the data: lunar laser ranging, rung 0 of the cosmic distance ladder — the only rung with no astrophysical model in the loop. Shown in the HUD ('distance known by: ...'). Its instantaneous-range line (~356,500-406,700 km real extremes, WIDER than our fixed-element 363,296/405,504) deliberately restates the fixed-elements residual from the outside.
origin: drafted by PCLA session termux-claude-907815e6 (Researches/cosmic-distance-measurement fold-in, entry 1 of N), adopted with minor edits by the pen-holder
deferred: the draft's optional _distance_ladder seed (incl. a conjectured H0-tension entry with GW-siren falsifier) is DEFERRED, not rejected: it renders nothing and its conjecture attaches to no body in a solar-system sky. It keeps until stars arrive — the draft itself named this out, and the founding rule (small trustworthy sky) decides.
note: first cross-repo fold-in, and the collaboration pattern held: PCLA drafted, Eve held the pen, the boundary rule made the scope call.

what: boundary-sky went PUBLIC and live on GitHub Pages: https://holbizmetrics.github.io/boundary-sky/ — verified serving (index 200, bodies.json 13 bodies + 2 meta entries, this log itself fetchable). A private falsifier register is almost a contradiction; from this record on, corrections happen in the open.
note: the append-only log, the two author-corrections it already contains, and the live P9 falsifier are now visible to anyone. The thesis, performing itself.

source: JPL Small-Body Database (1P/Halley), J2000 osculating elements; nucleus from the Giotto flyby (ESA, 1986)
evidence: independent Python check (replica of the page's own solveKepler): perihelion 0.5860 AU, aphelion 35.082 AU = a(1∓e) to 0 km, Kepler residual 8.9e-16 rad; whole-sky self-check still PASS across 13 orbits. Period 27,510 d (75.3 yr) matches Kepler III from a to ~2 days.
residual: fixed osculating elements; non-gravitational (sublimation-jet) forces omitted — the real driver of Halley's apparition-to-apparition timing drift; coma/tail not rendered (decorative); nucleus a true-scale ~5.5 km point
note: first COMET in the sky, and the founding case of the DERIVED tier: Edmond Halley predicted the 1758 return from Newton's gravitation and died in 1742 without seeing it confirmed. Retrograde (i=162°): it orbits the Sun backwards. Next perihelion 2061-07-28 is itself a falsifiable derived claim.

what: C/1995 O1 (Hale–Bopp) was staged (e=0.995086, a=186.4 AU, i=89.4° near-polar) but NOT shipped: the shared solveKepler (E=M initial guess, 20 undamped Newton iterations) DIVERGES for near-parabolic orbits — worst residual 6.75e11 rad against the <1e-9 gate. Halley (e=0.967), Pluto (e=0.249) and all planets pass; only e→1 breaks it.
correction_path: upgrade solveKepler with a near-parabolic-safe initial guess + safeguarding (bisection fallback or Markley's method), re-verify no planet regresses, THEN add Hale-Bopp — its data is otherwise ready (perihelion 0.916 AU, aphelion 371.9 AU).
note: a correction is the system working, not failing: the decidable self-check refused a body the renderer could not honestly place, despite surface-valid JSON. Logged so the constraint is not silently rediscovered (substrate-memory principle).

what: solveKepler replaced with a SAFEGUARDED Newton: the root of E - e*sinE = M lies within e of M (since E = M + e*sinE), giving the guaranteed bracket [M-e, M+e]; each iteration takes the Newton step but falls back to bisection whenever the step would leave the bracket, and iterates up to 100x. This cannot diverge. Verified against every shipped orbit PLUS Hale-Bopp: worst residual 2.0e-14 rad, perihelion and aphelion errors 0 km (gates 1e-9 rad / 1 km).
note: the divergence the finding refused is now a bounded, decidable pass. No planet regressed — the safeguarded step is identical to plain Newton wherever plain Newton was already converging. The self-check that blocked Hale-Bopp is the same self-check that now clears it.

source: JPL Small-Body Database (C/1995 O1), post-1997-perihelion osculating elements; nucleus ~30 km (HST + radio, Weaver/Fernández)
evidence: independent Python check on the real bodies.json with the new safeguarded solver: perihelion 0.916 AU, aphelion 371.9 AU = a(1∓e) to 0 km, Kepler residual 2.0e-14 rad; whole-sky self-check PASS across all orbits including this one.
residual: fixed osculating elements; non-gravitational forces omitted; the Jupiter-altered pre/post-1997 orbits are collapsed into a single post-encounter element set; coma/tail not rendered (decorative); nucleus a true-scale ~30 km point
note: the body that upgraded the solver. Near-polar (i=89.4°) and near-parabolic (e=0.995): perihelion inside Earth's orbit, aphelion 12x beyond Neptune. Shipped only after the machinery could honestly place it — the finding, the resolution, and this arrival are the whole substrate-memory loop closing in the open log.

source: NASA/JPL Jovian satellite mean elements; radii from Galileo/Voyager imaging
evidence: first CHILD-of-a-planet orbits after the Moon: bodyPosKm already composes parent+relative position, so no new machinery. Whole-sky self-check PASS across all 18 orbits (worst residual 2.0e-14 rad, peri/apo errors 0 km); every parent key resolves. The Io:Europa:Ganymede period ratios come out 2.007 and 4.044 — the 1:2:4 Laplace resonance emerging from the three MEASURED periods, not imposed.
residual: elements are referenced to Jupiter's Laplace/equatorial plane but applied directly in the ecliptic frame (the ~3.1° plane tilt + node omitted — orbits drawn a few degrees off, invisible at scale); node/periapsis set to 0 for these near-circular fast-precessing orbits (weakly pinned, and they do not move the perihelion the check tests); fixed two-body ellipses, no precession; the other ~90 jovian moons and the rings omitted
note: the four moons Galileo saw on 1610-01-07 — the observation that broke the geocentric sky — enter one tier-row from Halley's derived-tier triumph. They behave exactly like the shipped Moon: sub-pixel at full-system zoom, legible on focusing Jupiter. Callisto sits OUTSIDE the resonance and is the cold, dead, cratered one; the same measured periods that lock the inner three explain why.

source: NASA/JPL satellite mean elements; radii from Cassini/Huygens (Titan) and Voyager 2 (Triton)
evidence: whole-sky self-check PASS across all 20 orbits (worst residual 2.0e-14 rad, peri/apo errors 0 km); both parents (saturn, neptune) resolve. Same child-orbit machinery as the Galileans, no new code.
residual: Laplace-plane elements applied in the ecliptic frame (parent-pole tilt + node omitted, a few degrees off at invisible scale); node/periapsis zeroed (Titan weakly pinned; Triton near-perfect circle so periapsis is undefined); fixed two-body orbits, no precession; Titan's haze and Triton's geysers not rendered
note: two moons that each carry a fact in their orbit. Titan: second-largest moon, the only one with a thick atmosphere (~1.5 bar N2) and a methane liquid cycle — Huygens landed on it 2005. Triton: i=156.9° means it orbits Neptune BACKWARDS — a captured Kuiper Belt object, the retrograde orbit the fossil of the capture; coldest measured surface (~38 K) yet cryovolcanically alive; spiralling in toward a future ring. The tier system earns its keep again: the retrograde inclination is not a flourish, it is the measured evidence FOR the capture story, sitting in the same field Halley's i=162° uses.

source: Gaia DR3 (parallax 768.0665 mas); RA/Dec J2000; radius 0.1542 R_sun interferometric
evidence: first object beyond the solar system, and the first with NO orbit and NO parent that is not the Sun — a new renderer path (star_position → RA/Dec→ecliptic direction on a fixed shell). Direction verified: RA/Dec→ecliptic unit vector = (-0.3645, -0.7042, -0.6093), |v|=1.000000, ecliptic latitude -44.8° (Proxima is correctly far south of the ecliptic). Orbit self-check unaffected: 20 orbits still PASS (worst 2.0e-14 rad); the star is correctly NOT in the orbit loop (it has no orbit to check). JS syntax-checked (node --check).
residual: POSITION NOT TO SCALE — drawn on the decorative star-shell (2.5e11 km) because the true distance 4.02e13 km is 8898x Neptune's orbit and 67x beyond the far clip plane; marker size also not to scale. Only the DIRECTION and the stated distance are measured (derived-simplification tier on the drawn position, explicit correction_path: none possible in a single-scale scene — needs a future multi-scale/log-radius renderer).
note: rung 6, the first parallax. The distance is the point: measured by trigonometric parallax, rung 1 of the cosmic distance ladder, the geometric step directly above the Moon's LLR (rung 0) — two rungs now visible as a STRUCTURE in one sky, both model-free (one times light, one turns a triangle). Founding case honored in the data: Bessel's 1838 parallax of 61 Cygni, the first stellar distance ever measured — the parallax equivalent of Halley's derived-tier bet and Le Verrier's Neptune. One real star now sits among the decorative starfield in its true direction: the boundary between scenery and sky, drawn on the same shell and labeled.
